one_line_takeaway: A control-theory survey (not a security paper) that unifies AV contingency planning under a single hybrid-system formalism, showing internal faults favor Reactive fail-safe filters with formal guarantees while external interactive/semantic-OOD hazards increasingly favor Proactive branching methods that remain only empirically, not formally, verified.
key_finding: The paper's central analytical finding (Sec. 5.2), derived from its own qualitative classification of the surveyed literature (Table 2) rather than from experiments, is a structural divergence along two axes: (1) contingency source -- internal contingencies (actuator/sensor faults, which are abrupt and binary) are predominantly addressed with Reactive fail-safe mechanisms suited to low-latency stabilization, whereas external contingencies (interactive agents whose intent evolves over time) are increasingly addressed with Proactive branching methods that preserve behavioral recourse rather than triggering 'frozen robot' conservatism; and (2) verification maturity -- contingencies rooted in physical dynamics (kinematic hazards, interactive agents) are largely handled by methods carrying formal guarantees (invariant sets, CBFs, HJ reachability), while contingencies rooted in semantic understanding and OOD anomalies rely on empirical or probabilistic methods lacking formal proofs, a gap the authors label the 'Verification Gap.' The authors also formally show that branching time t_b in Proactive planning acts as a continuum parameter connecting the two paradigms: t_b -> 0 reduces to standard Reactive control, while t_b -> infinity reduces to fully robust (worst-case) control.
core_contribution: A survey/review paper (not an empirical study) that synthesizes fragmented literature on contingency planning for safety-critical autonomous vehicles (AVs) into a single 'logic-conditioned hybrid control framework.' The paper's own stated contributions are: (1) a rigorous mathematical formalization of a 'contingency' as a discrete, logic-conditioned modal transition in a stochastic hybrid dynamical system (Eq. 1-3), explicitly distinguishing contingencies (discrete, low-frequency, high-impact events) from continuous uncertainties like sensor noise; (2) derivation of two solution paradigms as computational approximations of the same underlying optimization problem -- Reactive Safety (Sec. 3: task-preserving runtime filters such as CBFs/HJ reachability, and task-terminating fail-safe/Minimal-Risk-Condition supervision), which assumes the discrete mode has already been resolved/detected, and Proactive Safety (Sec. 4: branching MPC formulated as a 'Risk-Constrained Branching Decision Process' with a non-anticipativity constraint over a scenario tree), which anticipates unresolved mode uncertainty; and (3) a comparative/taxonomic analysis (Table 1, Table 2) of the surveyed literature that identifies two structural divergences: internal system faults (actuator/sensor failures) are predominantly handled by Reactive mechanisms, while external interactive/agent-intent uncertainty increasingly drives adoption of Proactive branching; and physical/kinematic hazards are typically handled with formal guarantees (invariant sets, CBFs, HJ reachability) whereas semantic and out-of-distribution (OOD) anomalies rely on empirical/probabilistic validation, which the authors term a 'Verification Gap.'
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not a security paper and defines no adversary/attacker model. The paper's notion of 'hazard' is a discrete, low-probability physical/interactive event (sensor fault, actuator failure, occlusion, another driver's aggressive cut-in/merge, communication loss), modeled as a stochastic discrete-mode transition in a hybrid dynamical system, not a malicious actor exploiting a system. Where the paper uses the word 'adversarial' (abstract line 17, and Sec. 3.1.1 citing Bansal et al. 2017 / Fisac et al. 2018), it is in the game-theoretic/control-theoretic sense of worst-case disturbance in Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs reachability analysis, not an ML/cyber adversary crafting attacks. A grep of the full text found no discussion of adversarial machine learning attacks, cyber intrusion, spoofing, or exploit techniques; the only cybersecurity-adjacent references are a single citation to the ISO 'Road vehicles - safety and cybersecurity for automated driving' standard and one mention of 'black-box safety validation of cyber-physical systems' in the bibliography, neither of which is discussed in the paper's own analysis.
limitations: As a review, the paper reports no new experiments, benchmarks, or datasets of its own, so its claims (e.g., the Reactive/Proactive divergence pattern, the Verification Gap) are qualitative syntheses of the cited literature rather than independently validated results. The authors themselves flag open challenges rather than solved problems: (i) safety filters (CBF/HJ-based) are typically analyzed under simplified dynamics and idealized, instantaneous fault/contingency detection, whereas real faults are gradual/ambiguous (e.g., calibration drift), causing reactive latency or mode-switch chattering; (ii) CBFs require hand-crafted, validity-preserving barrier functions that are difficult to construct for complex driving scenarios and can render the safe set empty if invalid; (iii) HJ reachability suffers the curse of dimensionality (intractable beyond roughly 5D state without decoupling assumptions); (iv) learned/neural safety certificates (DeepReach, Neural CBFs) lack rigorous formal certification in most cases; (v) formal verification of the perception stack (e.g., VerifAI, robustness certification of neural classifiers) is limited to bounded L_p-norm perturbation models and does not extend to unbounded, semantically complex OOD distribution shifts; (vi) dynamic branching-time determination in Proactive planning remains computationally expensive for real-time deployment in dense multi-agent scenes and depends on well-calibrated upstream prediction uncertainty; and (vii) the field lacks standardized benchmarks for comparing contingency-planning methods.
